title: The Communist Manifesto
description: A revolutionary pamphlet calling for class struggle and the overthrow of capitalist society.
descriptionParagraphs: The Communist Manifesto compresses a sweeping theory of history into a short, urgent polemic about class struggle, capitalist transformation, and revolutionary possibility. Marx and Engels depict the bourgeoisie as an immensely dynamic force that has remade the world, even as they argue that capitalism generates exploitation, instability, and the collective power that could overthrow it.

What makes the text durable is its combination of diagnosis and slogan. It is both analytic and rhetorical, capable of striking formulations about markets, labor, and social change that have outlived the immediate moment of publication. Even critics read it because it captures the drama of capitalism's creative destruction with unusual speed, clarity, and antagonistic energy.

cover.alt: The Communist Manifesto cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A revolutionary pamphlet calling for class struggle and the overthrow of capitalist society.
banningParagraphs: The Communist Manifesto entered censorship debates as a political manifesto associated with communism, revolution, and class struggle. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around revolutionary politics and anti state.

The earliest event currently captured here is 19th century in Russia, where Tsarist censors prohibited circulation. Imperial authorities treated the manifesto as a threat to state order. Its ban history illustrates how governments respond to explicitly revolutionary theory.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
